faqs.a: It's a catch-all label for antidepressants that don't share the same mechanism as more familiar classes like SSRIs or tricyclics. They're grouped together more for what they aren't than for a single shared trait.

faqs.q: Does atypical mean the medication is weaker or rarely used?
faqs.a: No. The word atypical describes chemistry, not strength or quality, and several of these drugs are prescribed often. It just means they sit outside the older categories.

faqs.q: Is an atypical antidepressant the same as an atypical antipsychotic?
faqs.a: No. An atypical antipsychotic is a separate class used for different conditions. The shared word can cause confusion, but the two groups are not related.
